title: Repository Guidelines
text: R/ – exported R functions spatial/feature adjacency, kernels, helpers (e.g., neighbor_graph.R, knn_weights.R). src/ – Rcpp/Armadillo implementations (e.g., weight_funs.cpp); compiled objects may appear locally committed. tests/testthat/ – unit tests grouped feature (test-spatial-weights.R, test-commute-time.R, etc.). data-raw/ – scripts generate sample datasets; outputs land test_data/ data/. docs/ + _pkgdown.yml – pkgdown site config; man/ roxygen output; README.Rmd source README.md.

title: Repository Guidelines
text: R -q -e "devtools::document()" – regenerate roxygen docs NAMESPACE API changes. R -q -e "devtools::test()" – run testthat suite. R CMD build . – create source tarball. R CMD check neighborweights_*.tar.gz ---cran – full CRAN-style checks (slow authoritative). R -q -e "pkgdown::build_site()" – rebuild docs site locally. R -q -e "devtools::install()" – install package interactive use.

title: Repository Guidelines
text: Base R style 2-space indents, tabs; keep lines ≲100 characters. Functions use snake_case; S3 methods documented @method @export. Space around = arguments (sigma = 1); prefer explicit defaults. Keep roxygen examples minimal runnable; tag helpers @keywords internal. C++: follow existing Rcpp style, prefer const references, add brief // comments non-obvious logic.

title: Repository Guidelines
text: Place tests tests/testthat/ test-<topic>.R; mirror file names R/ feasible. Use testthat v3 expectations (expect_equal(..., tolerance = 1e-8) numeric results). Reuse fixtures test_data/; keep random seeds fixed generating data. Run devtools::test() pushing; run R CMD check tagging release.

title: Repository Guidelines
text: Commit messages: short, imperative summaries (e.g., “Fix example errors R CMD check”). PRs describe intent, key changes, verification commands; link issues available. Note API changes regenerated artifacts (man/, NAMESPACE, pkgdown); avoid committing local .o/.builds.

title: Repository Guidelines
text: Keep DESCRIPTION/NAMESPACE sync code changes; update Imports/LinkingTo adding new dependencies. touching Rcpp, run Rcpp::compileAttributes() documenting; clean stray binaries devtools::clean_dll() needed.

what: The problem: from points to a graph
title: Getting Started with adjoin
text: Many methods machine learning, spatial analysis, network science share common first step: similar data point neighbors? answer adjacency matrix — n×n sparse matrix entry (, j) holds similarity points j, -neighbor entries zero. adjoin constructs matrices three kinds inputs: Feature data (numeric matrices) — via k-nearest neighbor graphs Spatial coordinates — via radius- grid-based adjacency Class labels (factors) — via within-class connectivity output always neighbor_graph, lightweight object exposes consistent interface extracting adjacency matrix, computing graph Laplacian, inspecting edges.

title: Getting Started with adjoin
text: easiest entry point graph_weights(). Give data matrix, neighborhood size k, weight mode, returns ready--use neighbor_graph. graph_weights() searched 5 nearest Euclidean neighbors flower, converted distances similarities heat kernel (exp(−d²/2σ²)), symmetrized result. adjacency matrix stored sparse Matrix — 22,500 entries zero. Adjacency matrix reordered species. three diagonal blocks show flowers connect mostly within species. three diagonal blocks confirm graph recovering species structure raw petal sepal measurements — labels required.
code: set.seed(42) X  <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4])          # 150 flowers × 4 measurements ng <- graph_weights(X, k = 5,                     neighbor_mode = "knn",                     weight_mode   = "heat") A  <- adjacency(ng)                    # sparse 150×150 similarity matrix cat("nodes:", nvertices(ng), " | edges:", nnzero(A) / 2, "\n") #> nodes: 150  | edges: 508.5

title: Getting Started with adjoin
text: Every feature-similarity graph follows three-step pipeline. Step 1 — Prepare data. Rows observations; columns features. Step 2 — Build graph. Pass matrix graph_weights(). Step 3 — Use graph. Extract downstream methods need. Laplacian backbone spectral clustering, diffusion maps, graph-regularized learning. unnormalized symmetric-normalized forms available directly.
code: X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4]) dim(X) #> [1] 150   4 ng <- graph_weights(X, k = 5, weight_mode = "heat", sigma = 0.5) A      <- adjacency(ng)                  # sparse similarity matrix L      <- laplacian(ng)                  # L = D − A L_norm <- laplacian(ng, normalized = TRUE)  # I − D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}

title: Getting Started with adjoin
text: neighbor_graph named list: $G — igraph object holding full topology $params — construction parameters, kept reproducibility Three accessors cover common needs:
code: names(ng) #> [1] "G"      "params" str(ng$params, max.level = 1) #> List of 6 #>  $ k            : num 5 #>  $ neighbor_mode: chr "knn" #>  $ weight_mode  : chr "heat" #>  $ sigma        : num 0.5 #>  $ type         : chr "normal" #>  $ labels       : NULL nvertices(ng)      # number of nodes #> [1] 150 head(edges(ng))    # edge list as a character matrix #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,] "1"  "5"  #> [2,] "1"  "8"  #> [3,] "1"  "18" #> [4,] "1"  "28" #> [5,] "1"  "29" #> [6,] "1"  "38"

title: Getting Started with adjoin
text: weight_mode argument maps Euclidean distances similarity scores. Edge weight distributions three weight modes. three spread similarity scores continuously across (0, 1]. Soft weights preserve degree similarity, just presence — nearby neighbors score near 1, distant ones near 0. "binary" mode (shown) produces unweighted graphs every neighbor gets weight 1.
code: ng_norm <- graph_weights(X, k = 5, weight_mode = "normalized") ng_cos  <- graph_weights(X, k = 5, weight_mode = "cosine") ng_heat <- graph_weights(X, k = 5, weight_mode = "heat", sigma = 0.5)

title: Getting Started with adjoin
text: default, either point nominated neighbor edge included (type = "normal", union). Two alternatives trade sparsity confidence: Mutual graphs useful false positives costly — example, selecting reliable training neighborhood classifier.
code: # mutual: both must nominate each other (sparser, higher confidence) ng_mutual <- graph_weights(X, k = 5, weight_mode = "heat",                            type = "mutual")  # asym: directed — i→j does not imply j→i ng_asym   <- graph_weights(X, k = 5, weight_mode = "heat",                            type = "asym")  # edge counts: normal ≥ mutual c(normal = nnzero(adjacency(ng))       / 2,   mutual = nnzero(adjacency(ng_mutual)) / 2) #> normal mutual  #>  508.5  241.5

title: Getting Started with adjoin
text: repeated queries, build nnsearcher index query many times needed. Find neighbors every point: Search within subset (e.g., setosa flowers ): Search two groups (setosa vs. versicolor): Build neighbor_graph index: two-step approach useful need inspect raw distances committing kernel, want reuse index across multiple graph configurations.
code: searcher <- nnsearcher(X, labels = iris$Species) nn_result <- find_nn(searcher, k = 5) names(nn_result)   # indices, distances, labels #> [1] "labels"    "indices"   "distances" nn_setosa <- find_nn_among(searcher, k = 3, idx = 1:50) nn_cross <- find_nn_between(searcher, k = 3, idx1 = 1:50, idx2 = 51:100) ng2 <- neighbor_graph(searcher, k = 5, transform = "heat", sigma = 0.5)

title: Getting Started with adjoin
text: class labels available, class_graph() connects every pair -class points — creating fully connected block structure. class_graph adjacency iris. block fully connected class; edges cross species boundaries. class_graph objects neighbor_graph subclasses — every accessor (adjacency(), laplacian(), edges()) works . also support class-specific queries: building block supervised dimensionality reduction methods like LDA neighborhood component analysis.
code: cg <- class_graph(iris$Species) nclasses(cg) #> [1] 3 # within-class neighbors for every point wc <- within_class_neighbors(cg, X, k = 3)  # nearest between-class neighbors for every point bc <- between_class_neighbors(cg, X, k = 3)

title: Getting Started with adjoin
text: connect two different datasets, use cross_adjacency(): row holds heat-kernel similarities one query point 3 nearest matches reference set. foundation cross-modal retrieval, domain adaptation, inter-subject alignment.
code: X_ref   <- as.matrix(iris[1:100,  1:4])   # 100 reference points X_query <- as.matrix(iris[101:150, 1:4])  # 50 query points  # 50×100 sparse matrix: row i = query flower, col j = nearest reference C <- cross_adjacency(X_ref, X_query, k = 3, as = "sparse") dim(C) #> [1]  50 100

what: Normalizing for diffusion and random walks
title: Getting Started with adjoin
text: Raw adjacency matrices degree-imbalanced: high-degree nodes dominate. normalize_adjacency() row-normalizes produce Markov transition matrix: Row-stochastic matrices needed random walk analysis, compute_diffusion_kernel(), compute_diffusion_map().
code: A_raw  <- adjacency(ng) A_norm <- normalize_adjacency(A_raw)  # Confirm rows sum to 1 (stochastic matrix) range(Matrix::rowSums(A_norm)) #> [1] 0.5762124 1.3103125

title: Getting Started with adjoin
text: Spatial graphs — spatial_adjacency() builds graphs 2-D/3-D grid coordinates rather feature vectors; see ?spatial_adjacency. Diffusion — compute_diffusion_kernel() compute_diffusion_map() propagate information graph yield spectral embeddings. Label similarity — label_matrix() expand_label_similarity() create soft label-overlap matrices semi-supervised settings. Bandwidth selection — estimate_sigma() automatically picks heat kernel bandwidth data distribution.

what: When coordinates matter more than features
title: Spatial Neighbor Graphs
text: Many data sets come known spatial relationships: pixels image, voxels brain scan, sensors grid, measurements geographic locations. data question “neighbor?” physical answer — Euclidean distance coordinate space — rather statistical one derived feature vectors. adjoin provides focused set functions build adjacency matrices directly coordinate matrices. output always sparse Matrix interface feature-based graphs: adjacency(), laplacian(), normalization work way.

title: Spatial Neighbor Graphs
text: use 6 × 6 grid throughout vignette — small enough visualize, representative image brain-imaging data. spatial_adjacency() connects every point nearest spatial neighbors. Two parameters shape neighborhood: sigma — heat kernel bandwidth (larger = broader neighborhood) nnk — hard cap neighbor count (keeps matrix sparse) 36 grid points 8 heat-kernel-weighted neighbors within radius sigma × 3 = 4.5 grid units. Normalization symmetrizes matrix (D−1/2AD−1/2D^{-1/2} D^{-1/2}) edge weights comparable across nodes different numbers neighbors. Spatial neighbor graph jittered 6 × 6 grid. Thicker, darker lines higher-weight edges (nearer neighbours); lighter lines lower-weight (farther neighbours). Point colour encodes degree. Corner edge points fewer neighbors interior points. coordinates slightly irregular, edge weights also vary: thicker, darker lines connect closer pairs; faint lines connect pairs near neighbourhood boundary.
code: coords <- as.matrix(expand.grid(x = 1:6, y = 1:6))   # 36 points, 2 columns dim(coords) #> [1] 36  2 A <- spatial_adjacency(coords, sigma = 1.5, nnk = 8,                        weight_mode = "heat",                        include_diagonal = FALSE,                        normalized = TRUE) cat("size:", nrow(A), "x", ncol(A),     "| non-zero entries:", Matrix::nnzero(A), "\n") #> size: 36 x 36 | non-zero entries: 296 cat("symmetric:", isSymmetric(A), "\n") #> symmetric: TRUE

title: Spatial Neighbor Graphs
text: weight_mode controls spatial distance converted edge weight. Heat weights decay continuously distance; binary weights uniform within neighborhood radius. heat kernel produces smooth spectrum 0 1; binary produces single spike 1. Use "heat" distance matters, "binary" presence connection matters.
code: A_heat   <- spatial_adjacency(coords, sigma = 1.5, nnk = 8,                               weight_mode = "heat",                               include_diagonal = FALSE, normalized = FALSE,                               stochastic = FALSE) A_binary <- spatial_adjacency(coords, sigma = 1.5, nnk = 8,                               weight_mode = "binary",                               include_diagonal = FALSE, normalized = FALSE,                               stochastic = FALSE)

what: Effect of sigma on neighborhood size
title: Spatial Neighbor Graphs
text: Increasing sigma extends neighborhood softens weight decay, adding edges. nnk cap limits runaway growth. Tight sigma (0.8) leaves corner edge points connections; wide sigma (2.5) connects points richly.
code: A_tight <- spatial_adjacency(coords, sigma = 0.8, nnk = 27,                              weight_mode = "heat",                              include_diagonal = FALSE, normalized = TRUE) A_wide  <- spatial_adjacency(coords, sigma = 2.5, nnk = 27,                              weight_mode = "heat",                              include_diagonal = FALSE, normalized = TRUE)  c(tight_edges = Matrix::nnzero(A_tight) / 2L,   wide_edges  = Matrix::nnzero(A_wide)  / 2L) #> tight_edges  wide_edges  #>         238         534

title: Spatial Neighbor Graphs
text: spatial_smoother() converts spatial adjacency weighted averaging operator. Multiplying signal vector S replaces point’s value distance-weighted average neighbors. One pass spatial smoother turns Gaussian noise (left) smoothly varying field (right). heat kernel defines neighbor selection weight decay. Set stochastic = TRUE apply Sinkhorn–Knopp normalization produce doubly stochastic smoother (rows columns sum 1).
code: S <- spatial_smoother(coords, sigma = 1.5, nnk = 8, stochastic = FALSE)  set.seed(42) signal_raw      <- rnorm(36) signal_smoothed <- as.numeric(S %*% signal_raw)

title: Spatial Neighbor Graphs
text: spatial_laplacian() returns L = D − , D degree matrix. Multiplying signal L measures local curvature — much point deviates neighbors. Use normalized = TRUE symmetric form − D^{−1/2} D^{−1/2}, standard spectral clustering graph signal processing.
code: L <- spatial_laplacian(coords, dthresh = 4.5, nnk = 8,                        weight_mode = "binary", normalized = FALSE)  # Row sums of a Laplacian are zero by definition range(round(rowSums(L), 10)) #> [1] 0 0

title: Spatial Neighbor Graphs
text: coordinates feature observations, weighted_spatial_adjacency() blends two similarity sources. alpha parameter sweeps pure spatial weighting pure feature weighting. Adjacency heat maps three alpha values. Spatial-(left) shows clean block structure; feature-(right) looks noisier random features carry spatial signal. random features feature-matrix noisy. features carry genuine spatial signal — say, image intensities — blend rewards proximity similarity.
code: set.seed(42) features <- matrix(rnorm(36 * 5), nrow = 36, ncol = 5)  # random 5-d features  A_sp  <- weighted_spatial_adjacency(coords, features,                                     alpha = 0, sigma = 2, nnk = 8) A_mix <- weighted_spatial_adjacency(coords, features,                                     alpha = 0.5, sigma = 2, nnk = 8) A_ft  <- weighted_spatial_adjacency(coords, features,                                     alpha = 1, sigma = 2, nnk = 8)

title: Spatial Neighbor Graphs
text: bilateral_smoother() spatial smoother -weights neighbors different feature values. classic bilateral filter image processing: smooths within regions preserves sharp edges . Bilateral smoother (right) preserves hard boundary two signal regions; plain spatial smoother (left) blurs across . f_sigma parameter controls sensitive filter feature differences: smaller values enforce stricter edge preservation.
code: # Piecewise-constant signal with a hard boundary at x = 3.5 signal_field <- ifelse(coords[, "x"] <= 3, -1, 1) + rnorm(36, sd = 0.2) feature_mat  <- matrix(signal_field, ncol = 1)  B                <- bilateral_smoother(coords, feature_mat,                                        nnk = 8, s_sigma = 1.5, f_sigma = 0.5) signal_bilateral <- as.numeric(B %*% signal_field) signal_spatial   <- as.numeric(S %*% signal_field)   # plain spatial smoother

title: Spatial Neighbor Graphs
text: spatial_constraints() handles case spatial layout appears across multiple blocks — example, brain image grid measured across subjects sessions. builds combined constraint matrix encoding: Within-block connections: heat-kernel spatial similarity within block -block connections: correspondence matching locations across blocks shrinkage_factor balances two: 0 = within-block, 1 = -block. 32x32 constraint matrix two 4x4 spatial blocks. Diagonal blocks capture within-block spatial similarity; -diagonal blocks link corresponding locations across blocks. leading eigenvalue 1 construction. result normalized largest eigenvalue equals 1 — plugs directly spectral methods expect properly scaled operator. heterogeneous blocks block different feature observations, feature_weighted_spatial_constraints() extends per-block feature weighting.
code: coords_small <- as.matrix(expand.grid(x = 1:4, y = 1:4))  # 16-point grid  # Pass a list with one entry per block (same grid repeated for two subjects) S2 <- spatial_constraints(list(coords_small, coords_small), nblocks = 2,                            sigma_within   = 1.5,  nnk_within   = 6,                            sigma_between  = 2.0,  nnk_between  = 4,                            shrinkage_factor = 0.15)  dim(S2)   # 32 x 32: 2 blocks x 16 points #> [1] 32 32

title: Spatial Neighbor Graphs

Diffusion — compute_diffusion_kernel() propagates information adjacency matrix, spatial feature-based. Label constraints — expand_label_similarity() combines spatial proximity class labels semi-supervised methods. title: Build a flexible design-similarity kernel — design_kernel
text: Constructs positive semi-definite (PSD) kernel K design regressors encodes factorial similarity: level similarity, optional smoothness across ordinal levels, interaction structure via Kronecker composition. Works either directly cell space (one regressor per cell) effect-coded space (main effects, interactions), pulling K back user-specified contrast matrices.

title: Build a flexible design-similarity kernel — design_kernel

title: Build a flexible design-similarity kernel — design_kernel
text: factors named list, one entry per factor, e.g. list(=list(L=5,type="nominal"), B=list(L=5,type="ordinal", l=1.5)). ordinal, supply length-scale l (>0). Supported types: "nominal", "ordinal", "circular" (wrap-around distances). terms list character vectors; character vector lists factor names participate term. Examples: list("", "B", c("","B")) main , main B, :B. NULL, defaults singletons (main effects) full interaction. rho named numeric vector nonnegative weights term (names like "", "B", ":B"). NULL, defaults 1 term. Use rho0 identity term (see ). include_intercept Logical; TRUE, adds rho0 * K (small ridge / identity term). rho0 Nonnegative scalar weight identity term; default 1e-8. basis Either "cell" (default) "effect". "effect", must supply `contrasts`. contrasts named list contrast matrices factor, dimensions L_i x d_i. example: list(= contr.sum(5), B = contr.sum(5)). can also pass orthonormal Helmert. block_structure basis="effect", character vector naming sequence effect blocks include, e.g., c("","B",":B"). NULL, inferred `terms` `contrasts`. normalize One c("none","unit_trace","unit_fro","max_diag"). jitter Small diagonal added ensure SPD; default 1e-8.

title: Build a flexible design-similarity kernel — design_kernel
text: list elements: K PSD kernel matrix requested basis (cell effect) K_cell cell-space kernel matrix (always returned) info list containing levels, factor_names, term_names, basis, map     (T matrix effect basis), blocks

title: Build a flexible design-similarity kernel — design_kernel
text: kernel constructed using following principles: factor L_i levels, define per-factor kernel K_i (nominal ordinal) term S (subset factors), construct term kernel Kronecker product:         K_S = kronecker(K_i, K_j, ...) ,j S kronecker J matrices factors S Combine term kernels nonnegative weights rho[S] optionally add small ridge using effect coding, map cell kernel effect-space: K_effect = T' K_cell T

title: Create a Repulsion Graph — repulsion_graph
text: W input graph object. Can `Matrix` object (e.g., `dgCMatrix`) representing adjacency matrix, `neighbor_graph` object. Edge weights used `method="weighted"`. cg `class_graph` object defining class membership nodes `W`. Must dimensions `W`. method `character`. Specifies handle weights remaining (-class) edges: - `"weighted"` (default): Retains original weights `W`. - `"binary"`: Sets weight remaining edges 1. threshold `numeric`. threshold applied input graph `W` ** filtering class. Edges `W` weights strictly value discarded. Default 0. norm_fac `numeric`. normalization factor applied ** `method = "weighted"`. weights retained edges divided factor. Default 1 (normalization).

title: Create a Repulsion Graph — repulsion_graph
text: `repulsion_graph` object (inheriting `neighbor_graph`), representing filtered graph   containing -class edges.

title: Create a Repulsion Graph — repulsion_graph
text: function takes existing graph `W` (representing similarities, connections, etc.) filters based class labels. `class_graph` object `cg` provides binary adjacency matrix `1` indicates nodes belong ** class. taking complement (`!adjacency(cg)`), get mask `1` indicates nodes belong *different* classes. Element-wise multiplication (`W * !adjacency(cg)`) effectively removes within-class edges `W`. `method` argument controls whether remaining edge weights kept (`"weighted"`) converted binary indicators (`"binary"`). type graph useful focusing interactions ** distinct groups classes within larger network dataset.

title: adjoin 0.1.0
text: Initial CRAN release. Functions constructing spatial feature-weighted adjacency matrices. K-nearest neighbor graph construction heat, binary, cosine kernels. Class-based graph tools: class_graph, homogeneous_neighbors, heterogeneous_neighbors. Spatial constraint matrices multi-block data (spatial_constraints, feature_weighted_spatial_constraints). Diffusion kernels commute-time distances graphs. Label similarity matrices (binary_label_matrix, diagonal_label_matrix).
